name: Ernestine Anderson
abstract: Ernestine Anderson (November 11, 1928 – March 10, 2016) was an American jazz and blues singer. In a career spanning more than six decades, she recorded over 30 albums.

concerts.label: Carnegie Hall Jazz Band: Ella, Billie, Sarah and Carmen

works.t: Rosalie: How Long Has This Been Going On?

concerts.label: Body and Soul: The Music of Ella Fitzgerald

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: I hired her for a few jobs after that. I had -- casuals and I would always call her, because I enjoyed her singing so much.

about_quotes.speaker: Monk Montgomery
about_quotes.evidence: Ernestine Anderson quit... So when we went to Europe, Lionel Hampton had to get a replacement for the piano and the singer. So he got George Wallington on piano and Annie Ross as a singer, as a replacement for Ernestine...

about_quotes.speaker: Peter Appleyard
about_quotes.evidence: he’d made a record with Ernestine Anderson and she was on the gig with us. So we had to accompany Ernestine without Hank.
about_quotes.doc_title: Peter Appleyard interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, October 4, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Capp
about_quotes.evidence: Frank has played behind some of the great singers, including Ella Fitzgerald, Ernestine Anderson and Joe Williams.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Capp interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Annie Ross
about_quotes.evidence: I mean Ernestine Anderson, Shirley Horn, I mean those are some wonderful, wonderful singers.
about_quotes.doc_title: Annie Ross interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Ron Carter
about_quotes.evidence: Joe Close, piano player who ultimately went to join Ernestine Anderson out in Seattle
about_quotes.doc_title: Ron Carter — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
